# Introduction

The trajectory of artificial intelligence in medicine has undergone a transformation with few historical precedents. For decades, clinical AI remained largely confined to narrow, task-specific systems — rule-based expert systems, statistical classifiers applied to imaging datasets, and early natural language processing pipelines that required painstaking domain engineering. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) — deep neural architectures trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised objectives — shattered this paradigm. Beginning with GPT-3 in 2020, followed by the transformative release of ChatGPT in November 2022, and culminating in the rapid succession of GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA, and DeepSeek, these systems demonstrated that a single general-purpose model could reason across clinical domains, synthesize medical literature, generate coherent free text, and engage in nuanced diagnostic dialogue [@Lee2025ChatGPT; @Lim2026How]. The November 2022 launch of ChatGPT served as a decisive inflection point: within months, medical journals across every specialty published evaluations of LLM performance on board-style examinations, clinical vignettes, and guideline adherence tasks. The publication record reflects this rupture — a body of literature that was effectively nonexistent before 2023 has since grown into the thousands of peer-reviewed reports annually, spanning every domain from emergency medicine to oncology to geriatric care.

The foundational appeal of LLMs in clinical research lies in their capacity to process and generate natural language at scale, enabling tasks that were previously bottlenecked by human annotation labor or domain expertise. At the simplest tier of the capability spectrum, a clinician can pose an unstructured question in plain language and receive a contextually coherent answer. Studies benchmarking this zero-shot prompting approach across clinical tasks reveal a performance profile that is impressive yet uneven. In a cohort study of 300 intensive care discharge summaries, LLaMA-3.1 achieved 85% accuracy in primary diagnosis generation, followed closely by ChatGPT-4 (84.7%) and Gemini-1.5 (79%); however, ICD-9 code prediction accuracy collapsed across all models to between 14.6% and 42.6%, and hospital readmission risk stratification remained below 41% for most non-reasoning architectures [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating]. This performance gap between natural-language comprehension and structured clinical inference reflects a central tension in the field. Similarly, in a prospective study of NSTEMI cases administered to 1,106 emergency physicians, ChatGPT and Gemini correctly answered nine of ten guideline-based questions compared with a physician mean of 7.62 (±1.32), yet the real-world utility of such isolated accuracy metrics remains contested [@Yorgancolu2025Performance].

Recognizing the ceiling of simple zero-shot prompting, researchers and clinicians have developed a hierarchy of strategies to enhance LLM reliability. Prompt engineering — the deliberate structuring of input instructions, including chain-of-thought directives, few-shot exemplars, and role assignment — measurably improves performance on complex tasks. In a systematic evaluation of medication extraction from electronic health records (EHRs), chain-of-thought and few-shot prompting raised GPT-4o's F1 score for medication discontinuation classification toward 78.1%, while few-shot learning provided more consistent gains than chain-of-thought reasoning alone, particularly for open-source models such as Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct [@Shao2026Scalable]. For colorectal cancer staging from unstructured imaging reports, GPT-4 achieved T-stage extraction accuracy of 89%, N-stage accuracy of 90%, and M-stage accuracy of 85% using structured prompt engineering based on AJCC TNM criteria — matching or exceeding non-specialist data managers in most categories [@Kim2025Using]. Beyond prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) addresses a fundamental limitation of static LLMs: the inability to access current or institution-specific knowledge. A RAG system constructed from 40 nuclear medicine examination manuals at a single Japanese hospital, combining dense vector and BM25 sparse retrieval, demonstrated that GPT-4o with institutional document retrieval consistently outperformed GPT-3.5 on domain-specific question answering, with LLM-based evaluation metrics providing practical alternatives to expert scoring [@Fukui2025Evaluation]. An analogous GraphRAG approach applied to gestational diabetes mellitus offered a proof-of-concept for locally deployable, knowledge-grounded clinical reasoning [@Evangelista2026GraphRAGEnabled].

Domain-specific fine-tuning represents the next step along the capability spectrum, enabling models to internalize the distributional patterns of clinical text rather than relying on in-context adaptation. Fine-tuned models have demonstrated particular advantages in tasks requiring structured output generation. A fine-tuned LLM applied to the Modified Rankin Scale in EHR free text achieved high internal validity with performance metrics exceeding those of zero-shot general-purpose models, illustrating how task-specific adaptation can close the gap between benchmark performance and clinical deployability [@Silva2026Assessment]. An LLM-based system for validating ICD-10-CM codes, refined through iterative prompt engineering on the MIMIC-IV dataset of 865,079 assigned codes, found an overall positive predictive value of 84.6% (95% CI, 84.5–84.6%) for assigned codes, with principal diagnoses reaching 93.9% (95% CI, 93.7–94.1%), demonstrating the scalability of LLM-based validation pipelines [@Wang2026Validation]. At the frontier, agentic systems extend LLMs beyond single-turn inference by equipping them with tool use, external memory, multi-step planning, and verifier loops. @Liu2026Benchmarking benchmarked two agentic platforms — OpenManus (open-source, Llama-4-based) and the proprietary Manus system — across the AgentClinic, MedAgentsBench, and Humanity's Last Exam evaluation suites, finding that agentic architectures achieved 60.3% accuracy on AgentClinic MedQA and 30.3% on MedAgentsBench. Critically, although in-agent safeguards filtered 89.9% of hallucinations, residual hallucinations persisted, and resource demands exceeded baseline LLMs by more than tenfold in token consumption and twofold in latency — underscoring that autonomous clinical AI remains a nascent rather than mature technology.

The practical landscape of LLM applications in clinical research has nonetheless expanded dramatically across several domains. In clinical decision support, LLMs have been evaluated as reasoning partners for multidisciplinary teams: ChatGPT-4 achieved substantial agreement with an expert breast cancer multidisciplinary team (weighted κ = 0.712) compared with moderate agreement for ChatGPT-3.5 (κ = 0.600), with the largest discordance in aggressive or late-stage cases [@Tokoin2026Evaluating]. Free-tier LLMs evaluated against the 2024 ESC hypertension guidelines demonstrated high accuracy (82.5% for Claude 4.5 Sonnet; 80.0% for both ChatGPT-5.2 and Gemini 3 Flash), though a recurring safety bias toward over-medicalization was identified across all models [@Polus2026Adherence]. In EHR automation, AI scribing and ambient documentation systems have entered pragmatic trial evaluation: a University of Wisconsin implementation study reported a weighted median utilization rate of 65.4% for ambient AI note completion, with ICD-10 documentation accuracy initially declining from 79% (95% CI, 72–86%) to 35% (95% CI, 28–42%) following a note template change before recovering to baseline after workflow redesign [@Afshar2025A]. Across a meta-analysis of AI impact on physician productivity, AI-based EHR systems reduced documentation time by approximately 40%, while voice recognition and AI scribing reduced charting time by 28.8%, with radiology interpretation time savings ranging from 52.8% for lung lesions to 86.7% for pulmonary nodule review [@Lim2026How]. In clinical trial operations, the TrialMatchAI system — built on fine-tuned open-source LLMs within a RAG framework — demonstrated that 92% of oncology patients had at least one relevant trial retrieved within the top 20 recommendations, with over 90% accuracy in criterion-level eligibility classification for biomarker-driven matches [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI]. LLMs have also entered the meta-research process itself: GPT-4 variants evaluated on CONSORT-AI adherence assessment achieved overall consistency scores of 86.5% (95% CI, 82.5–90.5%), substantially exceeding GPT-3.5 (61.9%, 95% CI, 57.9–65.9%), though complex multi-criteria tasks exposed the limits of current autonomy [@Luo2025Using]. Holistic evaluation across 140 clinical tasks using the MedHELM benchmark further demonstrated that performance varies substantially across task types and model versions, with no single model dominating all clinical domains [@Bedi2026Holistic].

Against this backdrop of capability, a set of structural challenges threatens the responsible integration of LLMs into clinical research. Hallucination — the confident generation of factually incorrect or fabricated information — remains the most clinically consequential failure mode, and is not eliminated even in architecturally sophisticated agentic systems [@Liu2026Benchmarking; @Park2025Clinical]. In a standalone evaluation of GPT-4 on clinical data extraction, the model achieved an F1 score of only 0.63 for study inclusion tasks, compared with a physician F1 score of 1.00; neuro-symbolic hybrid approaches combining GPT-4 free-text extraction with rule-based expert verification reached F1 = 1.00 with 100% PSA value accuracy on 5,356 extracted data points, illustrating how architectural augmentation can substantially mitigate but not eliminate model limitations [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic]. Differential performance across demographic groups, languages, and health systems introduces systematic bias: a randomized pilot study of trauma education found that non-English prompting substantially degraded LLM performance, underscoring that accuracy benchmarked in English contexts may not generalize to multilingual clinical environments [@Gustafsson2026Integrating]. Knowledge cutoff dates mean that models trained before guideline updates may provide outdated clinical recommendations, a concern particularly acute in fast-moving areas such as oncology and cardiology [@Polus2026Adherence; @Tokoin2026Evaluating]. Regulatory frameworks have not kept pace with clinical deployment: jurisdictions across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific are developing LLM oversight mechanisms for medical devices, but no harmonized standard for validation, post-market surveillance, or mandatory hallucination reporting currently exists. The REFINE reporting guideline — an international consensus checklist for foundation and large language models in medical research — represents a nascent effort to standardize how LLM studies are reported, yet adoption remains voluntary [@Mese2026Reporting]. Finally, a systematic review of 39 benchmarks documented a knowledge–practice gap in which strong benchmark performance did not reliably predict behavior in real clinical workflows, a finding with direct implications for how evaluation evidence should inform deployment decisions [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice].

The rapid evolution of this field has outpaced synthesis. Existing reviews address isolated segments of the LLM-in-medicine landscape — evaluations within single specialties, comparisons of specific model versions on standardized examinations, or descriptive summaries of potential applications. No comprehensive review has yet traced the full arc from basic zero-shot prompting through prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, and autonomous agentic systems as an integrated methodological continuum applied to clinical research challenges. This gap is consequential: clinicians, research informaticists, and health system leaders making deployment decisions require an evidence synthesis that maps which LLM capabilities are mature, which remain experimental, and where validated use cases have been demonstrated alongside their associated failure modes. The present review addresses that gap by synthesizing evidence from 49 peer-reviewed studies published between 2024 and 2026, spanning the full spectrum of LLM architectures and clinical research applications.

This systematic review therefore addresses the following objectives:

1. To characterize the methodological spectrum of LLM deployment in clinical research, spanning zero-shot prompting, few-shot and chain-of-thought prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, domain-specific fine-tuning, and multi-agent agentic frameworks.
2. To synthesize quantitative evidence on LLM performance across key clinical research domains, including clinical decision support, EHR data extraction and automation, patient education, literature review automation, and clinical trial matching.
3. To identify consistent patterns of failure — including hallucination rates, systematic bias, and knowledge–practice gaps — across LLM deployment contexts.
4. To evaluate the current state of regulatory guidance, reporting standards, and validation frameworks applicable to LLM use in clinical research.
5. To derive evidence-based recommendations for the design, validation, and responsible implementation of LLM systems in clinical research settings.
